name: Hague Convention 1980 on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction
summary: The Hague Convention 1980 on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (formally Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction) is a global supranational meta-framework cross-border-child-abduction-resolution binding-treaty instrument adopted by the Hague Conference on Private International Law (HCCH) — structurally distinctive globally as the only modern universal multilateral binding-treaty framework operating specifically on cross-border-child-abduction-resolution framework (Hague Convention 1980 adopted 25 October 1980 + effective 1 December 1983 — formal universal multilateral binding-treaty framework establishing comprehensive cross-border-child-abduction-resolution framework, distinct from rest-of-modern-international-children's-rights frameworks which operate on broader best-interests + general-children's-rights frameworks, structurally distinctive globally as the only modern universal binding-treaty framework operating on formal-cross-border-child-abduction-resolution-specific framework + most directly relevant Hague framework to parental-alienation framework via cross-border-PA-intersection), as the central jurisdiction of the formal Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework that intersects with PA framework (Convention Article 13(b) framework providing for non-return exception where 'there is a grave risk that his or her return would expose the child to physical or psychological harm or otherwise place the child in an intolerable situation' — formal grave-risk exception framework operating substantial intersection with parental-alienation framework via formal PA-as-Article-13(b)-grave-risk-claim framework, structurally distinctive globally as the most directly-PA-relevant Hague Convention framework provision), as the central jurisdiction of the formal Central Authority cooperation framework (Convention Article 6 Central Authority designation framework + Article 7 Central Authority duties framework + Article 14 application to Central Authority framework — formal Central-Authority-cooperation framework, distinct from rest-of-modern-international-children's-rights frameworks operating without formal Central-Authority-cooperation framework), and as the central jurisdiction of one of the most distinctive modern cross-border-child-abduction-resolution case-law frameworks globally (multiple national-level apex court cases applying Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework to PA framework + ECtHR Neulinger and Shuruk v. Switzerland 6 July 2010 + UN Special Rapporteur on Violence Against Women Reem Alsalem Report Custody, Violence Against Women and Violence Against Children A/HRC/53/36 13 June 2023 critique of Article 13(b)-applied-to-domestic-violence framework — formal multi-jurisdictional Article 13(b) case-law framework, structurally distinctive globally as the most-developed modern cross-border-child-abduction-resolution case-law framework). The Hague Convention 1980 is binding upon 103 state parties as of 2026 — substantial state party + non-state-party framework reflecting widespread but not universal adoption framework. The Convention is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label, though formal Article 13(b) framework operates substantial intersection with parental-alienation framework via formal PA-as-Article-13(b)-grave-risk-claim framework. The Permanent Bureau of the Hague Conference on Private International Law is the apex monitoring framework body; national-level Central Authorities + national-level apex courts operate as administrative + judicial bodies. The Hague Convention 1980 is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label.

statutory_framework.citation: Hague Convention 1980 on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction
statutory_framework.title: Hague Convention 1980
statutory_framework.relevance: Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction adopted 25 October 1980 + effective 1 December 1983 — formal universal multilateral binding-treaty framework establishing comprehensive cross-border-child-abduction-resolution framework.

statutory_framework.title: Hague Convention 1980 Article 13(b)
statutory_framework.relevance: Hague Convention 1980 Article 13(b) framework providing for non-return exception where 'there is a grave risk that his or her return would expose the child to physical or psychological harm or otherwise place the child in an intolerable situation' — formal grave-risk exception framework operating substantial intersection with parental-alienation framework via formal PA-as-Article-13(b)-grave-risk-claim framework.

statutory_framework.title: Hague Convention 1980 Central Authority Framework
statutory_framework.relevance: Hague Convention 1980 Article 6 Central Authority designation framework + Article 7 Central Authority duties framework + Article 14 application to Central Authority framework — formal Central-Authority-cooperation framework.

statutory_framework.citation: Hague Convention 1980 Guide to Good Practice 2020 (Article 13(b))
statutory_framework.title: Hague Convention 1980 Guide to Good Practice Article 13(b)
statutory_framework.relevance: Hague Conference on Private International Law Guide to Good Practice 2020 on Article 13(b) — formal Hague Conference guidance framework for Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework application including PA-as-Article-13(b)-grave-risk-claim framework.

statutory_framework.citation: ECtHR Neulinger and Shuruk v. Switzerland Grand Chamber Judgment 6 July 2010
statutory_framework.title: ECtHR Neulinger v. Switzerland
statutory_framework.relevance: European Court of Human Rights ECtHR Neulinger and Shuruk v. Switzerland Grand Chamber Judgment 6 July 2010 — landmark Article 8 positive-obligations doctrine framework on cross-border-child-abduction + Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework interpretation.

apex_courts.name: Permanent Bureau of the Hague Conference on Private International Law (HCCH)
apex_courts.role: Apex monitoring framework body for Hague Convention 1980.

apex_courts.name: National-level Central Authorities
apex_courts.role: National-level Central Authorities — formal Central-Authority-cooperation framework operational bodies.

anonymisation_convention: Hague Convention 1980-related individual communications are anonymised per national-level court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Hague Conference on Private International Law founded
key_developments.description: Hague Conference on Private International Law HCCH founded 12 September 1893 — beginning of formal HCCH framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 adopted
key_developments.description: Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction adopted 25 October 1980 — formal universal multilateral binding-treaty framework establishing comprehensive cross-border-child-abduction-resolution framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 enters force
key_developments.description: Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction entered force 1 December 1983 — formal universal multilateral binding-treaty framework operational.

key_developments.title: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child adopted
key_developments.description: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child UNCRC adopted 20 November 1989 — universal multilateral framework complementary to Hague Convention 1980.

key_developments.title: Inter-American Convention 1989 adopted
key_developments.description: Inter-American Convention on the International Return of Children adopted 15 July 1989 — regional framework supplementary to Hague Convention 1980.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1996 adopted
key_developments.description: Hague Convention on the Protection of Children adopted 19 October 1996 — comprehensive framework supplementary to Hague Convention 1980.

key_developments.title: Neulinger v. Switzerland
key_developments.description: ECtHR Neulinger and Shuruk v. Switzerland Grand Chamber Judgment 6 July 2010 — landmark Article 8 positive-obligations doctrine framework on cross-border-child-abduction + Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework interpretation.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 Guide to Good Practice Article 13(b)
key_developments.description: Hague Conference on Private International Law Guide to Good Practice 2020 on Article 13(b) — formal Hague Conference guidance framework for Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework application.

key_developments.title: UN SR Custody Report 2023
key_developments.description: UN Special Rapporteur on Violence Against Women Reem Alsalem Report Custody, Violence Against Women and Violence Against Children A/HRC/53/36 13 June 2023 — formal UN Special Rapporteur critique of Article 13(b)-applied-to-domestic-violence framework.

structural_findings: Hague Convention 1980 operates a supranational universal multilateral binding-treaty framework — places Hague Convention 1980 in the global supranational-meta-framework cluster.

Only modern universal multilateral binding-treaty framework operating specifically on cross-border-child-abduction-resolution framework is structurally distinctive globally — formal-cross-border-child-abduction-resolution-specific framework + most directly relevant Hague framework to parental-alienation framework via cross-border-PA-intersection.

Central jurisdiction of formal Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework that intersects with PA framework is structurally distinctive globally — formal grave-risk exception framework operating substantial intersection with parental-alienation framework via formal PA-as-Article-13(b)-grave-risk-claim framework.
Central jurisdiction of formal Central Authority cooperation framework is structurally distinctive globally — formal Central-Authority-cooperation framework via Articles 6 + 7 + 14.

Central jurisdiction of one of most distinctive modern cross-border-child-abduction-resolution case-law frameworks globally is structurally distinctive globally — Neulinger 2010 + multiple national-level apex court cases + UN SR Custody Report 2023.
103 state party ratification framework is structurally distinctive globally — widespread but not universal adoption framework.
Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework + UN SR Custody Report 2023 critique of Article 13(b)-applied-to-domestic-violence framework is structurally distinctive globally.

1893 HCCH + 1980 Hague Convention + 1983 entry into force + 1989 UNCRC + 1989 Inter-American Convention + 1996 Hague Convention + 2010 Neulinger + 2020 Guide to Good Practice + 2023 UN SR Custody Report cross-border-child-abduction-framework-development sequence is structurally distinctive globally.

sources.title: Hague Conference on Private International Law (HCCH)

editorial_notes: Hague Convention 1980 on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction jurisdiction sidecar — supranational global cross-border-child-abduction-resolution universal multilateral binding-treaty meta-framework (Hague Convention 1980/1983 + Article 13(b) Grave Risk Exception + Articles 6-7-14 Central Authority Framework + Guide to Good Practice 2020 + Neulinger v. Switzerland 2010 + UN SR Custody Report 2023 + HCCH founded 1893 + UNCRC 1989 + Inter-American Convention 1989 + Hague Convention 1996). Only modern universal multilateral binding-treaty framework operating specifically on cross-border-child-abduction-resolution framework globally + most directly relevant Hague framework to parental-alienation framework via cross-border-PA-intersection + central jurisdiction of formal Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework that intersects with PA framework + central jurisdiction of formal Central Authority cooperation framework + central jurisdiction of one of most distinctive modern cross-border-child-abduction-resolution case-law frameworks globally + 103 state party ratification framework + Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework + UN SR Custody Report 2023 critique of Article 13(b)-applied-to-domestic-violence framework + 1893 HCCH + 1980 Hague Convention + 1983 entry into force + 1989 UNCRC + 1989 Inter-American Convention + 1996 Hague Convention + 2010 Neulinger + 2020 Guide to Good Practice + 2023 UN SR Custody Report cross-border-child-abduction-framework-development sequence. Hague Conference children's-frameworks triad complete (Hague 1980 + Hague 1993 + Hague 1996).

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator. NOTE: Article 13(b) grave-risk exception framework operates formal direct intersection with parental-alienation framework via formal PA-as-Article-13(b)-grave-risk-claim framework — the most-directly-PA-relevant Hague Convention framework provision.

Joins global + supranational + universal-multilateral-cross-border-child-abduction-resolution-binding-treaty cluster + Hague-Convention-1980-most-directly-PA-relevant-Hague-framework-via-Article-13b-grave-risk-globally-distinctive + Article-13b-grave-risk-exception-PA-intersection + Articles-6-7-14-Central-Authority-framework + Neulinger-2010 + UN-SR-Custody-Report-2023-Article-13b-critique + Guide-to-Good-Practice-2020-Article-13b + 103-state-party-ratification + complementary-to-Hague-1993-1996-Inter-American-1989-Council-of-Europe-1996-2003-UNCRC-CEDAW clusters within the corpus.
